_title: The Gap Between Stillness and Numbness
_raw_content: Most people who say they feel nothing believe they've found peace. But the nervous system knows the difference between stillness and shutdown. One is presence with an open channel. The other is a body that learned to leave before it could be hurt. Numbness is not calm. It is the oldest protection you have — so old it feels like home. Real stillness trembles. It is alive.
slide_1_hook: Calm is not always what it seems.

slide_2.text:
You stopped reacting.
You stopped feeling triggered.
You called it growth.

slide_3.text:
But you also stopped feeling joy.
Stopped feeling desire.
Stopped feeling anything
without effort.

slide_4.text:
There is a vast distance
between a nervous system
that is still
and one that has
simply left the body.

slide_5.text:
Numbness doesn't announce itself.
It wears the skin of peace.
It feels like you've finally
let go — but nothing
is being held at all.

slide_6.text:
Real stillness trembles.
It has a pulse.
You feel everything
and stay anyway.
That is regulation.
Not the absence of feeling —
the capacity to hold it.

slide_7_cta: Save this if your body went quiet and you need to know why.
caption:
I spent years confusing shutdown for serenity.

No reactions. No breakdowns. No mess.
I thought I had healed.

But I hadn't arrived in my body.
I had abandoned it so completely that the absence felt like stillness.

The nervous system is designed to protect you. And sometimes the most sophisticated protection it has is to make you feel nothing at all. Not because you're safe. Because it decided feeling was too expensive.

Healing was not learning to stay calm.
It was learning to feel again — and discovering I could survive it.

Real stillness is not empty.
It is full and soft and alive.
You'll know it because you can feel your own heartbeat without flinching.
